result:
At dawn the city remembers how to breathe. Steam lifts from manholes like tea from a chipped mug, and pigeons reorganize themselves along the parapets, bead-eyed accountants tallying the morning. I go out with a cracked umbrella I don’t need, a habit from wetter seasons, and walk toward the river that keeps its own counsel. The streets are still deciding who they want to be today: market, corridor, stage. Someone’s radio murmurs an old song to no one. I pass a bakery practicing warmth through glass, and a postal truck that already knows the names I’ve been trying to forget.

I have made a practice of noticing the almosts. The almost storm that never crosses the rooftops, the almost apology that dries unspoken at the edge of a tongue, the almost decision soft as lint in a pocket. There’s a comfort in hovering beside the hinge without closing the door. It feels like generosity until it starts to feel like refusal. The river is milk-glass today, the color of spoons left in water overnight. An egret stands like a letter too elegant to mail. I watch it remember the air is an invention, then lift into the oldest verb there.

In a window two floors up, a clockmaker rolls his shutters and sighs. I have watched his lamps assemble rainy afternoons for years. He keeps a tin of sugared almonds by the register and feeds them to children who ask about the smallest gears. Once, I took him a watch that had died in my sleep; he held it to his ear like a seashell, then smiled. It only needed a reminder, he said, and he tapped the case with his knuckle. I have been tapping the day for hours now. Perhaps it only needs the same reminder as well.

On the bench where moss writes its careful cursive, there is a playing card face down. I think of oracles and grocery lists, the way some answers hide beside mildew and graffiti. I turn it over, expecting a joker, but it is the queen of hearts, smudged with a thumbprint that is not mine. Once, this was part of laughter at a kitchen table, the flip and slide of futures, a bowl of grapes dissolving into shadows. Now it is an independent monarchy with no subjects, only weather. I slide it into my pocket because things dislike being unfinished too.

A bus groans by, antlers of advertisement catching sun. On its side a slogan promises a life uncreased by doubt if only I accept a password of numbers. I remember a friend who memorized every bridge in the city the summer his marriage collapsed. He said we should always keep a list of crossings ready, not for escape, but for perspective paid in steps. He taught me to pause at the center, to inventory the gravity beneath. Sometimes the river answers with a fish, sometimes with a shopping cart. Both gleam if the light is specific enough on certain days.

My grandmother used to mend light with lace curtains. She’d say the sun can be too blunt, better to let it arrive as a rumor, softened long enough to listen. She kept walnuts in her pockets for luck and stories on the stove. When I left home, she pressed a thimble into my palm and told me to sing to difficult tasks. I have sung to jars, tangled earbuds, brutally honest mirrors. I have forgotten the song for myself. The queen of hearts taps my thigh through denim. I think of calling someone I promised to stop calling this morning.

Instead I text my neighbor, the one with the lemon tree that believes in winter. Every year it produces bright misconceptions, small suns that blush and refuse to thaw. She names them for philosophers and lets them consider the frost. I tell her the egret has left, that the river is pretending to be porcelain, that I have rescued a queen from the sidewalk. She replies with a picture of a lemon named Simone rendered briefly golden by the kettle’s steam. We agree to meet at the fence later, to trade herbs, to practice being necessary to something alive today.

There is a man arguing gently with his dog about a stick. The dog wants the stick to be destiny; the man wants the walk to continue as planned. They compromise by carrying the stick together, monarchs of a shared absurdity. I am easy with this tenderness. I am less easy with the way a siren writes a diagonal across the moment. The world insists on its inventory of urgencies; I fold mine into my sleeve for now. On the bridge I stop as I was taught and look down. The river keeps secrets like a professional and an aunt.

When I was nine, I buried a note in a jar beneath the peach tree. It said please let me be kind and surprising and good at maps, and also get a dog. That summer the peaches arrived like lanterns, constellations within reach, and the dog never did. I forgot the jar until a storm pulled the roots sideways and it appeared among the crawling worms. The paper had undone itself into a moth-colored pulp. I thought, some requests are faithful even as they dissolve. I try to write them again today, with better spelling, with slower breath, without apology.

On the way home, the bakery opens its door and the day warms its hands. I buy a roll that crackles under its own small weather and thank the clerk by name. Names are bridges too. At the fence, my neighbor has clipped thyme and hope into paper cones. We lean there, pulse by pulse, not fixing anything, not leaving anything broken either. The queen of hearts peeks from my pocket, certain as a heartbeat in a dream. I hand it to her, a sovereign changed into offering. She laughs, names it Hannah, tucks it under Simone, and winter softens.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
